answer_length_cues.correct: The FAA Air Traffic Plans and Publications website, and the FAA Orders and Notices website.
No — control the traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled and along routes needed by emergency equipment.
From at least 30 minutes before the ETA until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after fuel is estimated exhausted.
The emergency equipment crew, the airport management, and the appropriate military agencies when requested by the pilot.
All aircraft receiving ATC service, all other aircraft with a filed flight plan or otherwise known to the ATC unit, and any aircraft known or believed to be subject to unlawful interference.
The RCC determines the airspace required for SAR operations, the ACC blocks that airspace until the RCC releases it, and an International NOTAM is issued describing the affected airspace.

En route: notify air carrier company radio stations for VFR company aircraft in the vicinity and FSSs adjacent to the emergency. Terminal: relay all information to the ARTCC where the emergency exists and disseminate as a NOTAM.
Approve it if the pilot has requested it and will not operate in Class A or B. The pilot's request serves as confirmation that weather conditions are adequate.

Plan for the aircraft to maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitudes. Because U.S. airspace is within 10 minutes of the clearance limit, the climb to the border-crossing altitude will commence at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.

Use EDST for strategic planning and conflict prediction, but maintain radar separation using actual radar track data. EDST is a strategic planning tool that uses current plans which may include unissued clearances.
Both require continued use of the affected tools with recognition that certain data may be affected—but the specific data that may be affected differs between the two situations.
Yes, if used in accordance with facility directives. Hold View is one of the five approved annotation methods, and no method requires supplementation by another.

Additional decision support tools must never be used to override probe results when the applicable separation standard is supported by probe and none of the other override conditions apply. The controller must not override in this situation.
Inform the pilot that ATC services are not provided for UAS operating at or below 400 feet AGL.
Continue using the VOR and inform the first aircraft that the second reported normal operations.
Continue controlling the formation as a single aircraft — the flight leader is responsible for separation within the formation during transition.
No — both the radar handoff AND frequency change must be COMPLETED. B hadn't accepted the handoff yet.
Ensure the B737 maintains at least 4 miles of separation until the threshold — wake turbulence separation must be maintained throughout the approach.
Yes — 15 minutes have not elapsed since the 1405Z report (1405 + 15 = 1420Z). The advisory requirement is still in effect.
No — the SOP transfer must be accomplished EACH time operational responsibility is transferred, regardless of duration or the relieving controller's experience.
Yes — the previous report was for the prior approach. A new approach requires a new reminder unless the pilot has already reported for this approach.
Notify the pilot of the deviation when workload permits — the standard is notification, not immediate action regardless of circumstances.
Do NOT issue the climb — it contradicts the RA descent. Support the RA maneuver and manage other traffic accordingly.
Only if the aircraft meets the criteria for excepted aircraft — non-RVSM aircraft are generally not permitted in RVSM airspace.
Do not issue the descent — it contradicts the TAWS pull-up maneuver. Support the alert and manage traffic around it.
Ensure the supervisor/CIC is notified — this is a 'Blue Lightning' event requiring supervisory notification.
Report it as soon as possible to the appropriate facility — this is a NAS component that may adversely affect air safety.
No — ETA revisions need forwarding only when the difference exceeds 3 minutes. 2 minutes is within the threshold.
Forward the departure time (as requested by the military office) but forward other VFR data only if requested by the pilot.

Delete the old altitude with the approved method, write the new altitude adjacent in the same space, and wait to line through the vacated altitude until the aircraft reports or is observed leaving it.

location_scaffolded.prompt: ['Same courses: angular difference less than 45 degrees', 'Crossing courses: 45 through 135 degrees inclusive', 'Opposite/reciprocal courses: greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive'] Two protected course paths intersect with an angular difference of 90 degrees. Which course relationship does the FIG 1-2-1 definition support? Classify a course relationship by angle band. Use the course-definition figure.

['Meaning to find: Approval Request', 'Use the abbreviation table as a bidirectional lookup'] In TBL 1-2-1, which abbreviation corresponds to Approval Request? Find the abbreviation paired with the meaning Approval Request. Use TBL 1-2-1 as a lookup table.

An ARTCC controller is preparing to forward information about an overdue IFR aircraft to the RCC. The controller has already forwarded the standard 11 items to the ARTCC (per 10-3-2). The controller now needs to ensure the RCC notification includes two additional items that the ARTCC notification does not require. INFORMATION TO BE FORWARDED TO RCC Which two items are unique to the RCC notification list compared to the ARTCC notification list? Identify the information items that differ between the ARTCC and RCC notification lists.
How do the VFR avoidance minima for ground missile emergencies compare to the IFR/SVFR minima and 10-7-3? Choose the best answer.

A facility is under scrutiny because its operational performance metrics show a pattern of applying miles-in-trail restrictions even during periods when TBFM is fully operational. When challenged, the traffic management unit says TBFM is treated as just one option among many equivalent tools, and MIT is often chosen because it requires less monitoring. DUTY RESPONSIBILITY Is the facility's approach consistent with 11-1-1? What should you do?

You are working Canadian airspace where control responsibility has been formally delegated to the FAA. A Canadian-specific procedure in Chapter 12 requires a different clearance phraseology than the standard FAA phraseology for the same operation. A trainee argues that you should use the FAA phraseology because 12-1-1 says to apply basic FAA procedures. APPLICATION Which phraseology should be used — the Chapter 12 Canadian phraseology or the standard FAA phraseology? Which procedure should you use?

A sector team is using the GPD to assess whether two aircraft will lose separation in 12 minutes based on their predicted trajectories. One controller suggests using the trajectory altitude instead of Mode C to verify one aircraft's altitude since the Mode C readout is intermittent. Another says the GPD is fine for assessing the future conflict picture but cannot replace Mode C for current altitude verification. USE OF GRAPHICS PLAN DISPLAY (GPD) Which proposed GPD use violates 13-1-13? Choose the prohibited GPD action.

A pilot reports a TCAS Resolution Advisory during a climb. Which abbreviation should the controller use to annotate this event in the Free Text Area? Using TBL 13-1-2 Miscellaneous Abbreviations, select the correct abbreviation for the situation described.

['ERAM ACL or DL Free Text Area entry', 'Abbreviation: CAF'] In TBL 13-1-1, what does the ERAM clearance abbreviation CAF mean? Use the clearance abbreviations table to decode the authorized abbreviation. Use the ERAM clearance abbreviation table.

An aircraft is holding at a published fix with a climb via clearance still active in the system. The aircraft needs holding instructions revised with a new altitude. A trainee wants to send the revised holding instructions via CPDLC because the holding message elements in TBL 14-2-2 support altitude assignment within holding clearances. SPECIFIC UPLINKS Can the revised holding instructions be issued via CPDLC? What should you do?
A pilot of a UAS operating at 350 feet AGL requests traffic advisories. What do you do? Per 2-1-1. What should you do?
An aircraft reports the ABC VOR is unreliable. You ask a second aircraft to check. The second aircraft reports the VOR is working normally. What should you do? Per 2-1-10. What should you do?
Two F-16s request to conduct formation practice. The flight lead states 'we'll take MARSA.' There is no LOA for this operation. Can MARSA be applied? Per 2-1-11. What should you do?

A formation of four F-16s is on approach. The flight leader reports they are beginning a transition from formation flight to individual control. The aircraft will maneuver to separate from each other. What is the controller's role? Per 2-1-13. What should you do?

An aircraft under your control is 5 miles from the adjacent sector boundary. You haven't coordinated the handoff yet. The receiving controller is busy. Can the aircraft cross the boundary? Per 2-1-14. What should you do?

Controller A initiates a radar handoff to Controller B. The data block is flashing on B's scope but B hasn't accepted it yet. A tells the aircraft to contact B's frequency. The pilot changes frequency. Has control been properly transferred? Per 2-1-15. What should you do?

You need to vector an IFR arrival through the tower's surface area at 1,500 feet to join the approach. Your facility has an LOA with the tower that waives individual coordination for IFR arrivals. What should you do? Per 2-1-16. What should you do?

A B737 is on a 5-mile final behind a B757 that landed 2 minutes ago. Required wake turbulence separation is 4 miles. Current radar shows the B737 at 4.5 miles from the threshold. Can you clear the B737 to land? Per 2-1-19. What should you do?
A B757 departed 2 minutes ago heading north. A C172 is departing on a crossing runway heading east. What must you include in the wake turbulence advisory? Per 2-1-20. What should you do?
Two aircraft are 5 miles apart and converging. The required separation is 3 miles. Do you need to issue a traffic advisory? Per 2-1-21. What should you do?

Put the initial radio-call elements in order.
Put the front end of the IFR clearance in order.
Put the airfile handling steps in order.
Put these delayed departures in the order their clearances should normally be issued.
Put the components of a degree-distance fix in order.

Place these communications-transfer steps in the correct procedural order.
Place these coordination items in the correct order.

context_light_prompts.back: Must NOT use CPDLC to clear aircraft out of holding (route uplink lacks clearance limit). Must NOT use CPDLC for holding instructions if aircraft has climb/descend via. Cancel vertical nav portion first. Per 14-2-4(c).
Military command prerogative; not invoked indiscriminately by individual units or pilots.
May only be applied to specified military IFR operations documented in an LOA or other appropriate FAA/military document.
Separate aircraft and issue safety alerts as required.
Issue to aircraft on your frequency when proximity may diminish to less than applicable separation minima; where no minima apply, issue when proximity warrants.
When it appears pilot actions constitute a pilot deviation, notify the pilot workload permitting.
Eligible aircraft are separated vertically by 1,000 feet in RVSM airspace.
Use automation procedures over nonautomation procedures when workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.
First come, first served as circumstances permit, except listed priority situations and without compromising safety.
Only when prompt compliance is required to avoid development of an imminent situation; a later non-expedite altitude restatement cancels the expedite instruction.
Only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.
Issue when aware an aircraft is in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Information about NAS components or flight conditions that may adversely affect air safety; report it as soon as possible to the appropriate facility or office.
Terminate interphone messages with your operating initials.
Monitor interphones and assigned radio frequencies continuously.
Full Route Clearance Necessary to the specified fix. If no fix is specified, full route clearance is required for the entire route.
DIRECT (degrees) DEGREES, (minutes) MINUTES (N/S), (degrees) DEGREES, (minutes) MINUTES (E/W).
Route segment that begins and ends with a published NAVAID, waypoint, fix, or airport.
Any altitude or flight level.
Before/upon approach clearance, before en route descent, prior to departure (terminal), and after changes.
Eight cardinal compass points and left/right base/downwind leg as appropriate.
Approach to one runway, landing on a parallel runway. Requires higher weather minima/MDA.
When the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track that will OBVIOUSLY cause deviation from protected airspace. Not speculative — the deviation must be obvious.
STARS may be used for: identifying aircraft with discrete beacon codes, maintaining target identity, and performing handoffs between controllers. All terminal STARS procedures apply to FUSION target.

30 degrees or more. Conditions: (1) pilot position report assures aircraft is within radar coverage/displayed area (except lost aircraft); (2) only ONE aircraft observed making turns; (3) IFR: heading away from increased-minima area or climb to highest MIA in area first.
If a TACAN/VORTAC is located within 6,000 feet of the radar antenna, it may be used as a reference fix without being displayed on the video map or map overlay.

1. Pilot requests. 2. Inform of reported tops OR no tops report available. 3. When necessary, issue alternative clearance for separation. 4. Reclear to MAINTAIN VFR-ON-TOP when pilot reports reaching it. Phraseology: CLIMB TO AND REPORT REACHING VFR-ON-TOP. IF NOT ON TOP AT (altitude), MAINTAIN (altitude), AND ADVISE.

Inform VFR-on-top aircraft when a report indicates non-compliance with 14 CFR 91.159(a). Phraseology: VFR-ON-TOP CRUISING LEVELS FOR YOUR DIRECTION OF FLIGHT ARE: ODD/EVEN ALTITUDES/FLIGHT LEVELS PLUS FIVE HUNDRED FEET.

1. Operating control tower. 2. Published name of CVFP and runway in clearance. 3. Ceiling at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA (or at/above MVA if following another aircraft). 4. Pilot affirms charted visual landmark or preceding aircraft in sight. USA/USN not applicable.

For authorized operators only (per LOA). Pilot must report airport in sight. Ceiling at least 500 ft above MVA/MIA, visibility 3 miles or greater. Phraseology: CLEARED RNAV VISUAL RUNWAY (number) APPROACH.

location_scaffolded.back: AJR: through channels to Systems Operations Services Director. Non-FAA/DoD: directly to AJV-P.
location_scaffolded.front: 1-1-9: AJR vs. non-FAA/DoD routing

location_scaffolded.back: Interpretation goes to AJV-P; clarification is returned to OSG for a written response to the requestor.
location_scaffolded.front: 1-1-9: interpretation vs. clarification routing

location_scaffolded.back: As soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act. Do not wait for the full supplementary list.
location_scaffolded.front: When does 10-2-1 say to start emergency assistance?

location_scaffolded.back: Determine the facility best able to provide service. If a frequency change is needed, advise the pilot of the reason, request contact with that facility, and inform the facility of the situation. If unable to communicate, relay information and clearances.
location_scaffolded.front: What steps does 10-2-7 require for a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty needing another facility?

location_scaffolded.back: Action taken by reporting facility and proposed action (f), and facility and person calling (a). The RCC list has 13 items; the ARTCC list has 11.
location_scaffolded.front: What two extra items does the 10-3-3 RCC notification list require that 10-3-2 ARTCC notification list does not?

location_scaffolded.back: They are used solely in oceanic airspace.
location_scaffolded.front: Where are the oceanic emergency procedures in Section 10-6 used?

location_scaffolded.back: Do NOT use GPD for: radar identification, position information, transfer of radar identification, radar separation, correlation, or point outs. Per 13-1-13(d).
location_scaffolded.front: GPD: six prohibited uses (13-1-13d)

location_scaffolded.back: The requirements of Chapter 8, Offshore/Oceanic Procedures, Section 2, Coordination ALSO apply. 13-2-6 establishes that automated coordination constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors—but this is IN ADDITION TO, not replacing, the Chapter 8 coordination requirements. Per 13-2-6.
location_scaffolded.front: ATOP automated coordination: what additional coordination framework applies beyond 13-2-6?

location_scaffolded.back: Because completing the mechanics of a handoff does not relieve the transferring controller of unresolved separation responsibility.
location_scaffolded.front: Why does 2-1-15 tie conflict elimination to transfer of control?

location_scaffolded.back: Because each set of circumstances must be evaluated on its own merit, and the safest action comes first.
location_scaffolded.front: Why does 2-1-2 emphasize good judgment instead of a fixed list for every situation?

location_scaffolded.back: They must become familiar with and stay aware of current weather information needed to perform ATC duties.
location_scaffolded.front: What weather-related responsibility do controllers have under 2-1-2?

location_scaffolded.back: Yes. The controller is NOT prohibited from issuing traffic advisories and safety alerts when notified of an RA. Only control instructions contrary to the RA are prohibited.
location_scaffolded.front: 2-1-28: may a controller issue traffic advisories or safety alerts to an aircraft responding to a TCAS RA?

location_scaffolded.back: 1. Distress 2. MEDEVAC/AIR EVAC/HOSP 3. Presidential 4. SAR 5. Interceptors 6. NAOC 7. FLYNET 8. Garden Plot (CARF) 9. SAMP 10. SCOOT 11. TEAL/NOAA 12. Flight Check 13. IFR over SVFR 14. NRP 15. Diverted 16. FALLEN HERO
location_scaffolded.front: Name 8+ priority handling categories under 2-1-4.

location_scaffolded.back: Pilots must comply with ATC clearances EXPEDITIOUSLY. Controllers must allow sufficient time for pilots to respond when issuing clearances. Per 2-1-5.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the mutual responsibility of pilots and controllers under 2-1-5?

location_scaffolded.back: Only when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given.
location_scaffolded.front: When do ETA revisions need to be forwarded as amended data under 2-2-11?

location_scaffolded.back: AF1 (Air Force), VM1 (Marine), VV1 (Navy), RR1 (Army), C1 (Coast Guard), G1 (Guard), EXEC1 (Commercial). Family uses EXEC1F for all services.
location_scaffolded.front: TBL 2-3-8, what are the aircraft identifiers for the President?

location_scaffolded.back: No more than seven alphanumeric characters.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the maximum length of an aircraft identity combination under 2-3-5?

location_scaffolded.back: To stop a lower-priority interphone message so a higher-priority message can be sent immediately.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the core purpose of the 2-4-11 interruption words?

location_scaffolded.back: The transmission ends with the controller's operating initials, not with a radio-style closing.
location_scaffolded.front: What is the required end-state of a normal interphone message under 2-4-13?

location_scaffolded.back: Use the separate digits of the frequency and insert the word point where the decimal occurs.

near_duplicates.left: How long should the Explanation of Changes pages be retained?
near_duplicates.right: How long should the Explanation of Changes pages be retained?

overloaded_backs.back: Obtain the call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness or risk if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if applicable, pilot's intent, and any request for assistance; notify the OMIC, OS, or CIC as soon as possible; the OMIC, OS, or CIC relays the information to the DEN.
overloaded_backs.front: What must be done when a death, illness, or other public health risk is reported on board an aircraft?

overloaded_backs.back: a. Emergency declared by pilot, facility personnel, aircraft officials, or system-generated transmission. b. Unexpected loss of radar AND radio communications with any IFR or VFR aircraft. c. Reports indicate a forced landing has occurred or is imminent, or operating efficiency is so impaired a forced landing will be necessary. d. Reports indicate the crew has abandoned or is about to abandon the aircraft.
overloaded_backs.front: What four declaration and communication triggers require considering an aircraft emergency (first half of the eight conditions)?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Pilot is IFR qualified and capable: request IFR flight plan and issue clearance. 2. Pilot is not qualified or capable — first offer VFR airport options. If pilot declares an emergency: provide radar assistance. 3. If pilot refuses to declare an emergency but the controller has determined the exact nature of the radar services the pilot desires: provide radar assistance.
overloaded_backs.front: What paths allow radar assistance to a VFR pilot who encounters IFR weather?

overloaded_backs.back: a. Avoid radio frequency changes except when necessary to provide a clear communications channel. b. Make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it will fly straight in IFR conditions. c. Have pilot lower gear and slow aircraft to approach speed while in VFR conditions. d. Avoid requiring a climb or descent while in a turn if in IFR conditions.
overloaded_backs.front: What four radar-assistance techniques concern frequency, turns, configuration, and combined maneuvers for non-IFR-qualified pilots?

overloaded_backs.back: Number of persons on board; fuel status; facility working the aircraft and frequency; last known position, how determined, time, estimated present position, and maximum range of flight based on remaining fuel and airspeed; position of other aircraft near the route of flight when requested; whether an ELT signal has been heard or reported near the last known position; and other pertinent information.
overloaded_backs.front: What aircraft status and search-related details must a terminal facility forward to the ARTCC for an aircraft that may require SAR?

overloaded_backs.back: Action taken by the reporting facility and proposed action; number of persons on board; fuel status; facility working the aircraft and frequency; last known position, how determined, time, estimated present position, and maximum range of flight based on remaining fuel and airspeed; position of other aircraft near the route of flight when requested; whether an ELT signal has been heard or reported near the last known position; and other pertinent information.
overloaded_backs.front: What status, action, and search-related details does the ARTCC forward to the RCC for an overdue or emergency aircraft?

overloaded_backs.back: An Alert Notice issued to all centers and Area B circuits, generally 50 miles on either side of the route of flight from the last reported position to destination. It must be issued before the RCC can begin search and rescue procedures, and the area may be expanded to cover the maximum range of the aircraft.
overloaded_backs.front: What is an ALNOT and what are its key operational parameters?

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Provide the most accurate latitude and longitude available using en route and terminal radar sensor data near the last known position. 2. If formal coordinates would cause undue delay, use any available method for an initial position, then follow up ASAP with formal coordinates. 3. Solicit assistance of other aircraft near the distressed aircraft's route if available. 4. Forward to the RCC or ARTCC as appropriate.
overloaded_backs.front: What is the two-tier procedure for providing last known position to the RCC and SAR teams?

overloaded_backs.back: (b) Do NOT use trajectory-based POSITIONS as a substitute for radar track position. (c) Do NOT use trajectory-based ALTITUDE in lieu of Mode C for altitude confirmation. (d) Do NOT use the GPD for six specific RADAR FUNCTIONS: radar identification, position information, transfer of radar ID, radar separation, correlation, or point outs. Per 13-1-13(b-d).
overloaded_backs.front: GPD: what are the three separate prohibitions in subparagraphs (b), (c), and (d), and what does each specifically forbid?

overloaded_backs.back: (1) Nonintersecting paths. (2) Intersecting flight paths with variable width protected airspace. (3) Reduction of Route Protected Airspace below FL 240. (4) Reduction of Route Protected Airspace at and above FL 240. (5) Same NAVAID: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN. (6) Same NAVAID: NDB. (7) Dead Reckoning. (8) Same Direction. (9) VFR Climb and Descent.
overloaded_backs.front: ATOP conflict probe: which separation standards does conflict probe NOT support? (9 categories)

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Distress 2. MEDEVAC/AIR EVAC/HOSP 3. Presidential 4. SAR 5. Interceptors 6. NAOC 7. FLYNET 8. Garden Plot (CARF) 9. SAMP 10. SCOOT 11. TEAL/NOAA 12. Flight Check 13. IFR over SVFR 14. NRP 15. Diverted 16. FALLEN HERO
overloaded_backs.front: Name 8+ priority handling categories under 2-1-4.

overloaded_backs.back: 1. Aircraft ID & type, 2. Departure point, 3. Original destination, 4. Position & time, 5. New destination, 6. ETA, 7. Remarks including change in fuel exhaustion time, 8. Revised ETA.
overloaded_backs.front: List the eight items to forward when a military aircraft changes destination.

overloaded_backs.back: Block 1: ACID. Block 2: CID. Block 3: number/type/equip. Block 4: CID if req'd. Block 5: beacon code. Block 6: coord fix. Block 7: overflight coord indicator (FDIO only). Block 8: ETA at coord fix. Block 8A: RA events (required when no voice recorder). Block 9: altitude/route. Blocks 9A-9C: optional. Blocks 10-18: per facility directive.
overloaded_backs.front: List the numbered blocks of the terminal flight progress strip.

overloaded_backs.back: Ground control must notify local control when: (1) a departing aircraft has been taxied to a runway other than the one previously designated as active; (2) an aircraft has been taxied to an intersection for takeoff. (3) When runways in use or aircraft using them are not visible from the tower or on radar, the controller releasing the aircraft must advise the other controller of the aircraft's location before the release.
overloaded_backs.front: What specific information must ground control notify local control of, and what must either controller advise before releasing an aircraft when visibility is limited?

overloaded_backs.back: Issue the latest braking action report for the runway in use to each arriving and departing aircraft. Include super or heavy reports for super or heavy aircraft when possible. If no report exists for the runway, issue an advisory. Advise the airport operator of qualifying braking action reports. Solicit PIREPs of runway braking action.
overloaded_backs.front: What actions must a controller take when Braking Action Advisories are in effect?

overloaded_backs.back: Aircraft identification; type of aircraft and equipment suffix; ETA or actual time and altitude over the clearance limit; clearance limit (when other than the destination airport) and any EFC issued; and the time, fix, or altitude when control responsibility transfers. Omission of the clearance limit and transfer details may be covered by a letter of agreement.
overloaded_backs.front: Before transfer of jurisdiction to approach control, what information is forwarded?

overloaded_backs.back: Known changes classified as special weather observations. All other items (altimeter setting, ceiling and visibility, airport conditions, and lost communication procedures) may be omitted after the first approach when repeated approaches are made and no change has occurred. Special weather observations must be issued as soon as possible; they need not be issued after they are included in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot states the appropriate ATIS broadcast code.
overloaded_backs.front: During repeated radar approaches with no changes, what is the one approach information item that cannot be omitted after the first approach under the omission rule?

overloaded_backs.back: When vectoring a departing IFR or missed approach aircraft with ISR not displayed, under one of three obstacle-separation conditions: maintain at least 3 NM laterally from obstacles while climbing 1,000 ft above them; increase separation until 3 NM exists; or no prominent obstacles within 10 NM of DER with uninterrupted climb to MVA/MIA within 10 NM.
overloaded_backs.front: When may a terminal controller vector below minimum IFR altitude?

overloaded_backs.back: Headings must permit final approach course interception on a track that does not exceed a 20-degree interception angle when the interception point is less than 2 miles from the approach gate or triple simultaneous approaches are in use, or 30 degrees (45 degrees for helicopters) when the interception point is 2 miles or more from the approach gate.
overloaded_backs.front: What constraint applies to headings assigned for final approach course interception?

overloaded_backs.back: When the reported ceiling at the primary airport is less than 1,000 feet and the operation is beneath a broken or overcast ceiling within the surface area. Standard VFR beneath the ceiling is not authorized; an SVFR clearance is required. (VFR operations ABOVE the ceiling are authorized under 14 CFR 91.155(a).)
overloaded_backs.front: When is a Special VFR clearance required for operations beneath a ceiling?
